question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a URL with reordered query parameters (e.g., ?order=desc&limit=10 vs ?limit=10&order=desc) so that cacheKeyGenerator() produces a different MD5 hash, preventing a legitimate user from receiving the cached transaction history and instead receiving a freshly-fetched (potentially different-ordered) response, causing inconsistent transaction ordering views?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cacheKeyGenerator() hashes req.originalUrl without normalizing URL-encoded characters (e.g., /api/v1/transactions%3Forder%3Dasc vs /api/v1/transactions?order=asc) to poison the cache with a response for a semantically identical but differently-encoded URL, causing subsequent users querying the canonical URL to receive stale or incorrect transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a crafted path containing mixed-case characters (e.g., /api/v1/Transactions?order=asc) to generate a distinct MD5 cache key, bypassing an existing cached response for /api/v1/transactions?order=asc and causing the cache to store a separate entry that later serves a different transaction ordering to users who hit that variant?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user append a URL fragment (e.g., /api/v1/transactions?order=asc#section) to req.originalUrl, causing cacheKeyGenerator() to produce a unique MD5 key that bypasses the existing cache entry and forces a fresh DB query, allowing the attacker to race-condition the cache update with a manipulated response ordering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of query parameter normalization in cacheKeyGenerator() by sending duplicate parameters (e.g., ?order=asc&order=desc) to generate a unique cache key, causing the cache to store a response with ambiguous transaction ordering that differs from what a single-parameter request would return?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit MD5 collision properties in cacheKeyGenerator() to craft two semantically different transaction history URLs that hash to the same MD5+'-v1' key, causing one user's transaction history query to be served the cached response of a different query with different ordering or timestamp filters?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with double-encoded characters in the URL (e.g., %2526 instead of %26) so that cacheKeyGenerator() produces a different MD5 hash from the canonical URL, causing the cache to store a separate entry that serves a different transaction history page to users who later request the double-encoded variant?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user add a trailing slash to a transaction history URL (e.g., /api/v1/transactions/) to generate a distinct MD5 cache key from /api/v1/transactions, causing the cache to store a separate entry and potentially serving a different transaction list ordering to users who hit the trailing-slash variant?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a crafted if-none-match header (CONDITIONAL_HEADER) matching the ETag of a cached transaction history response to force a 304 UNMODIFIED status, even when the underlying transaction history has been updated since the ETag was generated, causing the user to believe their cached (stale) transaction ordering is current?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ETag comparison logic (conditionalHeader === cachedResponse.headers[ETAG_HEADER]) by guessing or brute-forcing a valid ETag value from a previous transaction history response, then replaying it in an if-none-match header to suppress delivery of updated transaction history and keep a stale ordering visible to the client?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheCheckHandler() serves the cached response without re-validating the underlying data source, by timing a request to hit the cache immediately after a transaction ordering change is committed to the DB but before the cache TTL expires, causing the user to receive a stale transaction history with incorrect ordering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the cache-control header override (public, max-age=${redisTtl}) in responseCacheCheckHandler() to cause downstream CDN or proxy caches to serve a stale transaction history response for the full remaining Redis TTL, reorganizing the apparent transaction ordering for all users behind that CDN?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Object.assign(new CachedApiResponse(), redisValue) deserialization in responseCacheCheckHandler() by injecting a crafted JSON payload into Redis (via a separate Redis injection vector) that overrides the statusCode or headers fields, causing the cache to serve a manipulated transaction history response with altered ordering metadata?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the cache key is shared between HEAD and GET requests (cacheKeyGenerator() uses only req.originalUrl, not req.method) by sending a HEAD request to prime the cache with a response that lacks a body, causing subsequent GET requests to receive an empty or malformed transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of request method in cacheKeyGenerator() so that a HEAD request to /api/v1/transactions?order=asc populates the cache with a HEAD-specific response (no body), and a subsequent GET request to the same URL receives that cached response, resulting in an empty transaction history body being served?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the race condition between responseCacheCheckHandler() (cache miss path) and responseCacheUpdateHandler() by sending a burst of concurrent requests for the same transaction history URL, causing multiple DB queries with potentially different result sets (due to new transactions being committed) to race for cache storage, resulting in a non-deterministic transaction ordering being cached and served?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheUpdateHandler() caches 304 UNMODIFIED responses (isUnmodified check) by sending a conditional request that triggers a 304, causing the cache to store a response with headers merged from config.response.headers.default and res.locals[responseHeadersLabel], potentially overwriting the correct cache-control TTL with a longer one that keeps stale transaction history alive?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the header merging logic in responseCacheUpdateHandler() for 304 responses ({...config.response.headers.default, ...res.getHeaders(), ...(res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] ?? {})}) to cause a response with a manipulated ETag to be cached, so that future if-none-match requests using that ETag receive 304 responses even when transaction history has changed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the deletion of VARY_HEADER before caching (delete headers[VARY_HEADER]) in responseCacheUpdateHandler() to cause the cache to serve a response that was originally generated for a specific Accept-Encoding to a client with a different encoding preference, potentially corrupting the transaction history body if decompression fails?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the deletion of CONTENT_ENCODING_HEADER before caching in responseCacheUpdateHandler() combined with the negotiate() function's re-computation of encoding headers, to cause a mismatch between the stored compressed body and the content-encoding header served to the client, resulting in a garbled transaction history response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CACHE_CONTROL_REGEX (/^.*max-age=(\\d+)/) in getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault() by triggering a response with a crafted cache-control header value (e.g., max-age=999999) to extend the Redis TTL far beyond the intended value, causing stale transaction history to be served for an extended period and hiding newly committed transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the DEFAULT_REDIS_EXPIRY = 1 second fallback in getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault() by sending requests that cause the cache-control header to be absent or malformed, causing the cache to store transaction history for only 1 second and creating a high-frequency cache miss window where concurrent requests receive inconsistent transaction orderings from the DB?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the greedy regex match in CACHE_CONTROL_REGEX (/^.*max-age=(\\d+)/) by crafting a URL that causes the server to generate a cache-control header like 'public, max-age=0, s-maxage=3600', where the regex matches max-age=0 and returns 0, bypassing caching entirely and forcing every user to receive a fresh (potentially inconsistent) transaction history on each request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user send a crafted Accept-Encoding header (e.g., Accept-Encoding: identity) to negotiate() to force the server to serve the uncompressed body from cachedResponse.getUncompressedBody(), while simultaneously sending a request with Accept-Encoding: gzip to prime the cache with a compressed body, causing a content-length mismatch that truncates or corrupts the transaction history response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Negotiator library's encoding selection in negotiate() by sending a malformed Accept-Encoding header (e.g., Accept-Encoding: gzip;q=0) to force the server to serve the uncompressed body with an incorrect content-length header (cachedResponse.getUncompressedLength()), causing the client to receive a truncated transaction history?
